Its striking appearance comes from a vivid color contrast between a prominent red supergiant and dozens of surrounding hot blue-white stars, all packed into an area less than 20 light-years across. At only about 14 million years old it is one of the youngest naked-eye clusters known, and John Herschel described it as a casket of variously colored precious stones.

C95.extract: NGC 6025 is a bright, moderately rich open cluster in Triangulum Australe about 2,700 light-years away, containing around 60 member stars. It is one of the finest open clusters in the southern Milky Way accessible to binoculars, with a prominent central concentration of blue-white stars and a handful of slightly evolved yellowish members. Its estimated age of around 75 to 100 million years places it in the younger bracket of open clusters in this region of the galaxy.

C96.extract: NGC 2516 is a large, rich open cluster in Carina about 1,300 light-years away, containing around 300 confirmed members and comparable in appearance to the Pleiades but slightly farther and richer. Its estimated age of around 150 million years is notable for producing early studies comparing stellar rotation rates in the cluster to those in the Hyades and Pleiades to calibrate gyrochronology. A brilliant central red giant contrasts strikingly with the surrounding blue-white members.

C97.extract: The Pearl Cluster (NGC 3766) is a bright, rich young open cluster in Centaurus about 6,900 light-years away, known for the sparkling visual impression its densely packed blue-white stars give at low magnification. With an estimated age of only about 14 million years it is a very young cluster, and a 2013 study of its rapid rotators discovered a new class of periodic variable stars not predicted by standard stellar models. The cluster contains over 100 confirmed members and is a showpiece of the southern Milky Way.

C98.extract: NGC 4609 is a compact open cluster in Crux about 5,700 light-years away, lying directly adjacent to the Coalsack dark nebula (C99) in the sky. It contains around 40 stars in a moderately concentrated arrangement and is estimated to be around 45 million years old. The visual juxtaposition of this glittering cluster against the jet-black backdrop of the Coalsack makes it a striking wide-field pairing in photographs and binocular views.

C99.extract: The Coalsack is the most prominent dark nebula in the sky, a massive molecular cloud in Crux about 600 light-years away that appears as a strikingly dark, roughly oval void against the dense Milky Way star fields of the Southern Cross region. Spanning about 7 by 5 degrees on the sky, it is readily visible to the naked eye as one of the darkest patches in the entire Milky Way. Indigenous Australian peoples have long included the Coalsack in their astronomical traditions as part of the Emu in the Sky, a constellation defined by dark nebulae rather than stars.

C100.extract: IC 2944 is an active HII region and open cluster complex in Centaurus about 6,500 light-years away, surrounding the young star cluster IC 2948 whose O-type members ionize the surrounding gas. It is famous for its population of Bok globules, dark molecular condensations nicknamed Thackeray's Globules that are among the largest and most visually striking such objects known. Long-exposure narrowband images show an intricate landscape of glowing hydrogen gas, dark columns, and evaporating protostellar cocoons.

C101.extract: NGC 6744 is a large, loosely wound barred spiral galaxy in Pavo about 30 million light-years away, considered one of the closest analogs to the Milky Way in the local universe in terms of size, mass, and overall morphology. Its face-on orientation reveals sweeping spiral arms rich in star-forming regions and a prominent central bar, along with a nearby irregular companion NGC 6744A reminiscent of the Large Magellanic Cloud. Its low surface brightness and great apparent size of roughly 20 arcminutes make it a challenging visual target but a rewarding photographic one.

C102.extract: The Tarantula Nebula (NGC 2070) is the most luminous and massive star-forming region in the Local Group, lying about 160,000 light-years away in the Large Magellanic Cloud in Dorado. Its central super star cluster R136 contains dozens of stars exceeding 100 solar masses, including R136a1, currently considered the most massive known star at over 200 solar masses. If the Tarantula Nebula were as close as the Orion Nebula it would cast shadows on Earth, and it is bright enough to be occasionally visible to the naked eye as a small knot in the LMC.

C103.extract: NGC 2547 is a young, scattered open cluster in Vela about 1,400 light-years away, estimated to be only about 30 to 40 million years old. Its membership of around 50 confirmed stars is dominated by hot blue-white main-sequence stars, with several stars near the top of the main sequence still contracting toward the zero-age main sequence. Spitzer Space Telescope observations detected warm dust around several solar-type members, evidence for protoplanetary disk evolution at this young age.

C104.extract: NGC 362 is a bright, compact globular cluster in Tucana about 27,700 light-years away, appearing in the same wide-field view as the Small Magellanic Cloud and the globular 47 Tucanae (C106). It is unusually metal-rich for such a large, massive cluster, and its stellar population analysis reveals evidence of multiple stellar generations. Its high central concentration makes it difficult to resolve in small telescopes but a beautiful tight ball of stars in larger instruments.

C105.extract: NGC 4833 is a loosely concentrated globular cluster in Musca about 21,200 light-years away, notably reddened by foreground interstellar dust that limits its apparent brightness compared to its true luminosity. It orbits on a retrograde path close to the Galactic plane, where it experiences strong tidal forces, and its outer regions are less populated than expected, possibly due to ongoing tidal stripping. Its loose concentration class makes individual stars resolvable in moderate-aperture telescopes.

C106.extract: 47 Tucanae (NGC 104) is the second brightest globular cluster in the sky and the finest in the southern hemisphere after Omega Centauri, lying about 14,850 light-years away on the outskirts of the Small Magellanic Cloud in Tucana. Despite its apparent proximity to the SMC it is about 60,000 light-years closer, lying in the Milky Way's halo. Its dense, rich core resolves into hundreds of individual stars in any telescope, and it contains a remarkable concentration of millisecond pulsars thought to form through stellar collisions in its dense interior.

C107.extract: NGC 6101 is a loosely concentrated globular cluster in Apus about 49,500 light-years from Earth, one of the more remote Milky Way halo globulars. Studies have proposed it may be associated with the Gaia-Enceladus accretion event based on its retrograde orbit and chemical abundances. Its low central concentration and distant location make it a faint, poorly resolved object in moderate apertures, appearing as a granular haze rather than a resolved stellar system.

C108.extract: NGC 4372 is a large, loosely concentrated globular cluster in Musca about 19,000 light-years away, heavily reddened by foreground interstellar dust. Its enormous apparent angular diameter of around 20 arcminutes makes it one of the largest apparent globulars in the sky, but the combined effect of reddening and its low concentration means it is poorly detected in small instruments and best appreciated in wide-field photographs. It is noticeably metal-poor compared with most Milky Way globulars, placing it among the oldest and most primordial stellar systems known.

C109.extract: NGC 3195 is a bright planetary nebula in Chamaeleon about 6,700 light-years away, one of the most southerly planetary nebulae observable from most populated latitudes. Its nearly circular disk is compact but readily visible in moderate-aperture telescopes under clear southern skies, appearing as a pale blue-gray disk at high magnification. It is one of the farthest-south objects in the Caldwell catalog and inaccessible to most northern hemisphere observers even at the most southern continental observing sites.

NGC40.extract: The Bow-Tie Nebula (NGC 40) is a bright planetary nebula in Cepheus about 3,500 light-years away, whose elongated double-lobed structure is visible in deep images. Its central white dwarf is one of the coolest known for a planetary nebula, with a surface temperature of only around 50,000 Kelvin. The nebula's reddish color in photographs comes primarily from ionized nitrogen and hydrogen emission.

NGC129.extract: NGC 129 is an open cluster in the constellation Cassiopeia. It was discovered by William Herschel in 1788.

NGC136.extract: NGC 136 is an open cluster in the constellation Cassiopeia. It was discovered by William Herschel on November 26, 1788.

NGC157.extract: NGC 157 is an intermediate spiral galaxy in the constellation of Cetus, positioned about 4° east of the star Iota Ceti. This galaxy can be viewed from suburban skies using a moderate-sized telescope.

NGC185.extract: NGC 185 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy about 2.05 million light-years away in Cassiopeia and a satellite of the Andromeda Galaxy, paired in the sky with the nearly identical NGC 147. Unlike most dwarf ellipticals, it contains a small population of relatively young stars and a nuclear concentration of dust, indicating that some star formation has occurred within the last billion years. A carbon star population gives its nucleus an unusually warm color compared with NGC 147.

NGC205.extract: Messier 110 is the second-largest satellite galaxy of the Andromeda Galaxy, a dwarf elliptical about 2.69 million light-years away. Unusually for a galaxy of its type, it contains patches of dark dust and evidence of relatively recent star formation, suggesting a more complex history than typical dwarf ellipticals. Its faint, elongated glow is visible alongside M31 and M32 in binoculars under dark skies.

NGC225.extract: NGC 225 is an open cluster in the constellation Cassiopeia. It is located roughly 2,200 light-years from Earth. It is about 100 to 150 million years old.

NGC247.extract: NGC 247 is a moderately inclined dwarf spiral galaxy in Cetus about 11.1 million light-years away, a member of the Sculptor Group. It is famous for a prominent dark void region in its northern disk with no detectable star formation, and its large apparent size of over 20 arcminutes and low surface brightness make it a photographic rather than a visual target. Cepheid variable stars in NGC 247 have been used to refine the distance to the Sculptor Group.

NGC278.extract: NGC 278 is an isolated spiral galaxy in the northern circumpolar constellation of Cassiopeia, near the southern constellation boundary with Andromeda. It lies at a distance of approximately 39 megalight-years from the Milky Way, giving it a physical scale of 190 ly (58 pc) per arcsecond.

NGC381.extract: NGC 381 is an open cluster of stars in the northern constellation of Cassiopeia, located at a distance of approximately 3,120 light-years from the Sun. Credit for the discovery of this cluster was given to Caroline Herschel by her brother William in 1787, although she may never have actually seen it.

NGC404.extract: NGC 404 is a field galaxy located about 10 million light years away in the constellation Andromeda. It was discovered by William Herschel in 1784, and is visible through small telescopes.

NGC436.extract: NGC 436 is an open cluster located in the constellation Cassiopeia. It was discovered on November 3, 1787, by William Herschel. It was described by Dreyer as a "cluster, small, irregular figure, pretty compressed."

NGC488.extract: NGC 488 is a face-on spiral galaxy in the constellation Pisces. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 13 December 1784.

NGC524.extract: NGC 524 is a lenticular galaxy in the constellation Pisces. It is at a distance of about 90 million light-years away from Earth.

NGC559.extract: NGC 559 is a moderately rich open cluster in Cassiopeia about 3,700 light-years away, containing around 150 member stars. It lies in a rich Milky Way field, and its estimated age of roughly 160 million years places it in middle age for an open cluster. Several slightly evolved yellow stars are mixed in with the cluster's dominant blue-white population.

NGC584.extract: NGC 584 is an elliptical galaxy in the constellation Cetus. The galaxy was discovered on 10 September 1785 by the German-British astronomer William Herschel.

NGC596.extract: NGC 596 is an elliptical galaxy in the constellation Cetus. The galaxy lies 65 million light years away from Earth, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 596 is approximately 60,000 light years across.

NGC598.extract: The Triangulum Galaxy is a spiral galaxy 2.73 million light-years away in Triangulum, the third-largest member of the Local Group after the Milky Way and Andromeda. Its face-on disk spans about 60,000 light-years and contains NGC 604, one of the largest known HII star-forming regions at over 1,500 light-years across. Under excellent dark skies it is just detectable with the naked eye, though its low surface brightness makes it a challenging binocular object.

NGC613.extract: NGC 613 is a barred spiral galaxy located 67 million light years away in the southern constellation of Sculptor. The galaxy was discovered by German-English astronomer William Herschel on 9 December 1798, then re-discovered and catalogued by Scottish astronomer James Dunlop on 5 August 1826.

NGC615.extract: NGC 615 is an unbarred spiral galaxy seen edge-on located in the constellation Cetus. It is located at a distance of circa 70 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 615 is about 75,000 light years across.

NGC637.extract: The cluster was discovered on 9 November 1787 by German-born English astronomer William Herschel. It is located in the Perseus Arm of the Milky Way, at a distance of approximately 7,045 light years from the Sun.

NGC650.extract: The Little Dumbbell Nebula is a small planetary nebula in Perseus about 2,500 light-years away, and the visually faintest object in the entire Messier catalog. Its two bright lobes form a dumbbell or barbell shape, while a dimmer surrounding halo makes it a bipolar nebula viewed nearly side-on. The central white dwarf is one of the hottest known, with a surface temperature estimated around 88,000 Kelvin.

NGC654.extract: NGC 654 is an open cluster in the constellation Cassiopeia. It was discovered by William Herschel in 1787.

NGC659.extract: NGC 659, also known as the Yin-Yang Cluster is an open cluster in the Cassiopeia constellation. It was discovered by Caroline Herschel in 1783.

NGC720.extract: NGC 720 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Cetus. It is located at a distance of about 80 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 720 is about 110,000 light years across.

NGC752.extract: NGC 752 is a large, scattered open cluster in Andromeda about 1,300 light-years away, containing around 60 confirmed member stars spread across a wide area best suited to binoculars or a rich-field telescope at low power. At an estimated age of 1.1 to 1.4 billion years, it is one of the older open clusters known to have retained a coherent membership. Several evolved red giant stars are present, making it useful for stellar evolution studies of intermediate-age clusters.

NGC772.extract: NGC 772 is a large unbarred spiral galaxy approximately 106 million light-years away in the constellation Aries. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 29 November 1785.

NGC779.extract: NGC 779 is a spiral galaxy seen edge-on, located in the constellation Cetus. It is located at a distance of circa 60 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 779 is about 70,000 light years across.

NGC908.extract: NGC 908 is an unbarred spiral galaxy in the constellation Cetus. It was discovered on 20 September 1786 by William Herschel. This galaxy is 56 million light years away from Earth. It is the main galaxy in the NGC 908 group, which also includes NGC 899, NGC 907, and IC 223.

NGC936.extract: NGC 936 is a barred lenticular galaxy in the constellation Cetus. It is at a distance of about 60 million light-years away from Earth.

NGC1022.extract: NGC 1022 is a barred spiral galaxy located approximately 68 million light years away in the equatorial constellation of Cetus. It was discovered on September 10, 1785, by William Herschel. NGC 1022 is a member of the Cetus-Aries group of galaxies.

NGC1052.extract: NGC 1052 is an elliptical galaxy in the constellation Cetus. It was discovered on January 10, 1785 by the astronomer William Herschel. It is a member of the eponymous NGC 1052 Group.

NGC1245.extract: NGC 1245 is an open cluster in the constellation Perseus. It was discovered by William Herschel on 11 December 1786.

NGC1342.extract: NGC 1342, sometimes also known as the Stingray Cluster, is an open cluster in the constellation Perseus. It was discovered by William Herschel on 28 December 1799.

NGC1407.extract: NGC 1407 is an elliptical galaxy in Eridanus. It is at a distance of 76 million light-years from Earth.

NGC1444.extract: NGC 1444 is a small open cluster of stars in the northern constellation of Perseus, about 2-1⁄4° to the northwest of 43 Persei. The cluster has sixty members of seventh magnitude or fainter, and is better appreciated in larger telescopes.

NGC1501.extract: NGC 1501 is a planetary nebula with a complex structure, located in the northern constellation of Camelopardalis. It has the proper names Camel's Eye Nebula and the Oyster Nebula.

NGC1502.extract: NGC 1502 is a young open cluster of approximately 60 stars in the constellation Camelopardalis, discovered by William Herschel on November 3, 1787.

NGC1513.extract: NGC 1513 is an open cluster of stars in the northern constellation of Perseus, positioned 2° SSE of the faint star Lambda Persei. The same telescope field contains the clusters NGC 1528 and NGC 1545.

NGC1545.extract: NGC 1545 is an open cluster in the constellation Perseus. It was discovered by William Herschel on December 28, 1790.

NGC1647.extract: NGC 1647 is an open cluster in the constellation Taurus. It contains nearly 90 stars and it lies at a distance of 550 parsec.

NGC1664.extract: NGC 1664 is an open cluster in the constellation of Auriga. It contains stars with a total of around 640 solar masses with a tidal radius of 43 ly (13.2 pc).

NGC1817.extract: NGC 1817 is an open cluster of stars in the constellation Taurus. It was discovered by English astronomer William Herschel in February 1784.

NGC1907.extract: NGC 1907 is an open star cluster around 5,200 light years from Earth. It contains around 300 stars and is around 500 million years old. With a magnitude of 8.2, it is visible in the constellation Auriga.

NGC1961.extract: NGC 1961 is a spiral galaxy in the constellation Camelopardalis. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 3 December 1788.

NGC1964.extract: NGC 1964 is a barred spiral galaxy in the constellation Lepus. The galaxy lies 65 million light years away from Earth, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 1964 is approximately 150,000 light years across.

NGC2022.extract: It was first observed by William Herschel on December 28, 1785, who described it as: considerably bright, nearly round, like a star with a large diameter, like an ill-defined planetary nebula. In medium-sized amateur telescopes it looks like a small grayish patch of light.

NGC2129.extract: NGC 2129 is an open cluster in the constellation Gemini. NGC 2129 is a very young cluster whose age has been estimated at 10 million years.

NGC2158.extract: NGC 2158, also known as OCL 468, Lund 206 or Melotte 40, it is an open cluster of stars located around 11,000 light years from Earth in the constellation of Gemini. It is of intermediate age being around 2 billion years old.

NGC2194.extract: NGC 2194 is an open cluster in the constellation Orion. The cluster is located about 10,000 light years away from Earth. It is rich and moderately concentrated. The cluster lies 33 arcminutes west-northwest of 73 Orionis.

NGC2204.extract: NGC 2204 is an open cluster of stars in the Canis Major constellation. It was discovered by the German-English astronomer William Herschel on 6 February 1785.

NGC2232.extract: NGC 2232 is a bright open star cluster in the equatorial constellation of Monoceros, centered on the star 10 Monocerotis. It is located in the Gould Belt close to the Orion Nebula cluster, at a mean distance of 1,060 ly from the Sun.

NGC2244.extract: NGC 2244 is the brilliant young open cluster at the centre of the Rosette Nebula, containing about 200 stars formed from the surrounding molecular cloud roughly 4 million years ago. The most massive members are luminous O-type giants whose ultraviolet radiation and stellar winds carved the central cavity of the Rosette Nebula and still power its glowing gas. At low magnification the cluster is a striking loose scattering of bright blue-white stars set against the glowing nebular backdrop.

NGC2266.extract: NGC 2266 is an open cluster of stars in the constellation of Gemini. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 7 December 1785.

NGC2281.extract: NGC 2281, also known as the Broken Heart Cluster, is an open cluster of stars in the northern constellation of Auriga. It was discovered by English astronomer William Herschel on March 4, 1788, and described as a, "cluster of coarsely scattered pretty [bright] stars, pretty rich".

NGC2354.extract: NGC 2354 is an open cluster in the constellation Canis Major. It lies 2 degrees southwest from NGC 2362 and northeast of Delta Canis Majoris. About 15 member stars are visible through binoculars.

NGC2355.extract: NGC 2355 also known as NGC 2356, is an old open cluster in the constellation Gemini.

NGC2360.extract: NGC 2360 is a rich, moderately concentrated open cluster in Canis Major about 3,700 light-years away, containing roughly 80 confirmed members. Its estimated age of around one billion years makes it relatively old for an open cluster, and several evolved red giant members stand out against the predominantly blue-white stars. At low to moderate magnification it is a distinctive compressed grouping in a rich Milky Way field.

NGC2362.extract: NGC 2362 is a compact, bright young open cluster in Canis Major about 5,000 light-years away, centered on the luminous fourth-magnitude blue supergiant Tau Canis Majoris. At only about 4 to 5 million years old it is one of the youngest known open clusters, and nearly all of its hot blue-white members are still descending the main sequence from their formation. Its tight, compressed appearance at low magnification makes it one of the most striking young clusters in the winter sky.

NGC2371.extract: NGC 2371-2 is a dual lobed planetary nebula located in the constellation Gemini.

NGC2419.extract: The Intergalactic Wanderer (NGC 2419) is one of the most remote globular clusters in the Milky Way halo, lying about 300,000 light-years from the Galactic Center in Lynx, nearly as far as the Magellanic Clouds. Its great distance makes it a test case for dark matter models and the outer limit of the Milky Way's gravitational reach. Despite its immense distance, it is one of the most massive Milky Way globulars, containing around one million stars.

NGC2420.extract: NGC 2420 is an open cluster in the constellation Gemini. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 19 November 1783. The cluster is about two billion years old and it is located 10,000 light years away.

NGC2422.extract: Messier 47 is a bright, coarse open cluster of about 50 stars in Puppis, just 1,600 light-years from Earth. Its brightest members are a mix of blue-white and orange giant stars, visible as colored pinpoints in binoculars. The cluster is roughly 78 million years old and forms a visual pair with M46 just 1.5 degrees to the east.

NGC2438.extract: NGC 2438 is a planetary nebula in the southern constellation of Puppis. Parallax measurements by Gaia put the central star at a distance of roughly 1,370 light years.

NGC2506.extract: NGC 2506 is a rich, compact old open cluster in Monoceros about 10,700 light-years away, containing several hundred stars. Its estimated age of around 1.5 to 2 billion years makes it one of the older open clusters known to have survived dynamical disruption. The cluster's main-sequence turnoff at evolved subgiant stars makes it a useful reference point for stellar isochrone calibrations.

NGC2509.extract: NGC 2509 is an open cluster in the constellation of Puppis. It was discovered on 3 December 1783 by William Herschel. It was described as "bright, pretty rich, slightly compressed" by John Louis Emil Dreyer, the compiler of the New General Catalogue.

NGC2527.extract: NGC 2527 is an open cluster in the constellation Puppis. It was discovered by William Herschel on December 9, 1784.

NGC2539.extract: NGC 2539 is an open cluster in the constellation Puppis, located at the north edge of the constellation. It was discovered by William Herschel on January 31, 1785. It is a moderately rich cluster and with little central concentration, with Trumpler class II1m.

NGC2548.extract: Messier 48 is a large, scattered open cluster of about 80 stars in Hydra, 1,500 light-years away. Messier recorded it at incorrect coordinates and the true identity of this object was not confirmed until 1959 when it was matched to NGC 2548. It spans about 23 light-years and has an estimated age of 300 million years.

NGC2567.extract: NGC 2567 is an open cluster in constellation Puppis. It was discovered on March 4, 1793, by William Herschel.

NGC2613.extract: NGC 2613 is a spiral galaxy in the southern constellation of Pyxis, next to the western constellation border with Puppis. It was discovered by the German-born astronomer William Herschel on November 20, 1784.

NGC2627.extract: NGC 2627 is an open cluster of stars in the constellation Pyxis. It was discovered on March 3, 1793 by German-British astronomer William Herschel.

NGC2655.extract: NGC 2655 is a lenticular galaxy in the constellation Camelopardalis. It is at a distance of 60 million light years from Earth.

NGC2681.extract: NGC 2681 is a lenticular galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major. The galaxy lies 50 million light years away from Earth, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 2681 is approximately 55,000 light years across.

NGC2768.extract: NGC 2768 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation of Ursa Major. It is at a distance of 65 million light years from Earth.

NGC2775.extract: NGC 2775 is a bright early-type spiral galaxy in Cancer about 55 million light-years away, displaying a very smooth outer disk and a slightly chaotic inner spiral structure visible in deep images. It has an unusually large and luminous nucleus relative to its disk, suggesting its spiral arms have been largely exhausted of gas through past star formation. A handful of dwarf companion galaxies orbit nearby in the Cancer I Group.

NGC2782.extract: NGC 2782 is a peculiar spiral galaxy that formed after a galaxy merger in the constellation Lynx. The galaxy lies 75 million light years away from Earth, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 2782 is approximately 100,000 light years across.

NGC2787.extract: NGC 2787 is a barred lenticular galaxy approximately 24 million light-years away in the northern constellation of Ursa Major. It was discovered on December 3, 1788 by German-born astronomer William Herschel.

NGC2859.extract: NGC 2859 is a barred lenticular galaxy located some 83 million light years away in the constellation Leo Minor. The morphological classification is (R)SB(r)0+, where the S0+ notation indicates a well-defined physical structure that is lacking in visible spiral arms.

NGC2950.extract: NGC 2950 is a lenticular galaxy in the northern constellation of Ursa Major, about 50 million light years from the Milky Way and receding with a heliocentric radial velocity of 1,329 km/s. It was discovered in 1790 by the Anglo-German astronomer William Herschel.

NGC2964.extract: NGC 2964 is an intermediate spiral galaxy located in the constellation Leo. It is located at a distance of about 60 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 2964 is about 60,000 light years across.

NGC2974.extract: NGC 2974 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Sextans. It is located at a distance of circa 90 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 2974 is about 90,000 light years across.

NGC2976.extract: NGC 2976 is a peculiar dwarf galaxy in the northern constellation of Ursa Major. Dreyer described it as, "bright, very large, much extended 152°, star involved".

NGC2985.extract: NGC 2985 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of circa 70 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 2985 is about 95,000 light years across.

NGC3034.extract: The Cigar Galaxy is a starburst galaxy about 11.4 million light-years away in Ursa Major, undergoing an explosive burst of star formation triggered by gravitational interaction with M81. Its central starburst region forms stars at a rate ten times higher than the Milky Way, and supernovae and stellar winds drive a spectacular superwind of ionised gas extending thousands of light-years above and below the disk. In 2014 it hosted SN 2014J, one of the nearest Type Ia supernovae observed in modern times.

NGC3147.extract: NGC 3147 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Draco. It is located at a distance of about 130 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3147 is about 140,000 light years across.

NGC3166.extract: NGC 3166 is a lenticular galaxy in the constellation Sextans. The galaxy lies about 65 million light years away from Earth, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 3166 is approximately 105,000 light years across.

NGC3169.extract: NGC 3169 is a spiral galaxy about 75 million light years away in the constellation Sextans. It has the morphological classification SA(s)a pec, which indicates this is a pure, unbarred spiral galaxy with tightly-wound arms and peculiar features.

NGC3193.extract: NGC 3193 is an elliptical galaxy in the constellation Leo. The galaxy lies about 90 million light years away from Earth, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 3193 is approximately 80,000 light years across. It was discovered by William Herschel on March 12, 1784.

NGC3198.extract: NGC 3198, also known as Herschel 146 is a barred spiral galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 15 January 1788.

NGC3226.extract: NGC 3226 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy that is interacting with the spiral galaxy NGC 3227. They were both discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 15 February 1784.

NGC3227.extract: NGC 3227 is an intermediate spiral galaxy that is interacting with the dwarf elliptical galaxy NGC 3226. The two galaxies are one of several examples of a spiral with a dwarf elliptical companion that are listed in the Atlas of Peculiar Galaxies.

NGC3242.extract: The Ghost of Jupiter (NGC 3242) is a bright, roundish planetary nebula in Hydra about 1,400 light-years away, named for its resemblance in a small telescope to a gas giant planet with a slightly oval, blue-green disk. Its concentric shells of ionized gas include a bright inner oval and a fainter outer halo discovered in deeper imaging, and a pair of faint ansae similar to the Saturn Nebula extend from its outer halo. At roughly 2 arcminutes across, it is large enough to show obvious disk morphology even in modest apertures at medium magnification.

NGC3245.extract: NGC 3245 is a lenticular galaxy in the constellation Leo Minor. It was discovered by William Herschel on April 11, 1785.

NGC3277.extract: NGC 3277 is a spiral galaxy in the constellation of Leo Minor. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 11 April 1785.

NGC3294.extract: NGC 3294 is a spiral galaxy in the constellation Leo Minor. It was discovered by William Herschel on Mar 17, 1787.

NGC3310.extract: NGC 3310 is a grand design spiral galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major. It is a starburst galaxy and it is likely that NGC 3310 collided with one of its satellite galaxies about 100 million years ago, triggering widespread star formation.

NGC3377.extract: NGC 3377 is an elliptical galaxy in the constellation Leo. It is a member of the M96 Group and is about 26 Mly away, with a diameter of approximately 40 000 ly.

NGC3379.extract: Messier 105 is the brightest elliptical galaxy in the Leo I Group, about 32 million light-years away in Leo. Unlike most ellipticals, it shows evidence of relatively recent low-level star formation in its core and harbors a supermassive central black hole of roughly 200 million solar masses. It is usually photographed alongside its companions NGC 3371 and NGC 3373 in a compact group.

NGC3384.extract: NGC 3384 is an elliptical galaxy in the constellation Leo. The galaxy was discovered by William Herschel in 1784 as part of the Herschel 400 Catalogue.

NGC3395.extract: NGC 3395 is a peculiar spiral galaxy in the constellation Leo Minor. The galaxy lies about 55 million light years away from Earth, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 3395 is approximately 35,000 light years across.

NGC3412.extract: NGC 3412 is a barred lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Leo. It was discovered on April 8, 1784, by the astronomer William Herschel.

NGC3414.extract: NGC 3414 is a lenticular galaxy in the constellation Leo Minor. It was discovered by William Herschel on April 11, 1785.

NGC3432.extract: NGC 3432 is an edge-on spiral galaxy that can be found in the northern constellation of Leo Minor. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on March 19, 1787.

NGC3486.extract: It has a morphological classification of SAB(r)c, which indicates it is a weakly barred spiral with an inner ring and loosely wound arms. This is a borderline, low-luminosity Seyfert galaxy with an active nucleus.

NGC3489.extract: NGC 3489 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Leo. It is located at a distance of about 30 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3489 is about 30,000 light years across.

NGC3504.extract: NGC 3504 is a barred spiral galaxy in the constellation Leo Minor. It has a Hubble distance corresponding to 88 million light-years and was discovered by William Herschel in 1785.

NGC3556.extract: The Surfboard Galaxy is a barred spiral in Ursa Major seen nearly edge-on, about 45 million light-years away. Its ragged, mottled profile lacks a prominent central bulge, giving it a rough cigar shape broken by dark dust lanes and scattered bright HII regions. It lies just 48 arcminutes from the Owl Nebula (M97), and both are a dramatic pairing in a wide-field photograph.

NGC3593.extract: NGC 3593 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Leo. It has a morphological classification of SA(s)0/a, which indicates it is a lenticular galaxy of the pure spiral type.

NGC3607.extract: The galaxy was discovered March 14, 1784 by William Herschel. Dreyer described it as "very bright, large, round, very much brighter middle, 2nd of 3".

NGC3608.extract: NGC 3608 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Leo. It was discovered by William Herschel on March 14, 1784.

NGC3610.extract: NGC 3610 is an elliptical galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major. It was discovered on 8 April 1793 by German-British astronomer William Herschel.

NGC3613.extract: NGC 3613 is an elliptical galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major. It was discovered by the astronomer William Herschel on 8 April 1793. NGC 3613 is the center of a cluster of galaxies, and has an estimated globular cluster population of over 2,000.

NGC3619.extract: NGC 3619 is an unbarred lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It was discovered by the astronomer William Herschel on March 18, 1790.

NGC3621.extract: NGC 3621 is a disk spiral galaxy about 22 Mly (6.7 Mpc) away in the equatorial constellation of Hydra. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 17 February 1790.

NGC3626.extract: NGC 3626 is a lenticular galaxy in Leo about 70 million light-years away, notable for its counter-rotating gas disk: the interstellar gas in the inner disk rotates in the opposite direction to the galaxy's stars. This counter-rotation is a signature of gas acquired from an external source, likely a cannibalized dwarf galaxy in the relatively recent past. A modest ring of star formation has been triggered by this accreted gas, giving the nucleus a slightly bluer color than typical for a lenticular.

NGC3631.extract: NGC 3631 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of about 35 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3631 is about 60,000 light years across.

NGC3640.extract: NGC 3640 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Leo. It is located at a distance of circa 75 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3640 is about 90,000 light years across.

NGC3665.extract: NGC 3665 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of circa 85 million light-years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3665 is about 85,000 light years across.

NGC3675.extract: NGC 3675 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of about 50 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3675 is about 100,000 light years across.

NGC3686.extract: NGC 3686 is a spiral galaxy in the constellation Leo. It has two spiral arms and a weak bar.

NGC3726.extract: NGC 3726 is a barred spiral galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of circa 45 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3726 is about 85,000 light years across.

NGC3729.extract: NGC 3729 is a barred spiral galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of circa 65 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3729 is about 60,000 light years across.

NGC3810.extract: NGC 3810 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Leo. It is about 50 million light years from Earth, and estimated to be about 60,000 light years in diameter. William Herschel discovered it on 15 March 1784.

NGC3893.extract: NGC 3893 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of circa 50 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3893 is about 70,000 light years across.

NGC3898.extract: NGC 3898 is a spiral galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major that was discovered by William Herschel on April 14, 1789.

NGC3900.extract: NGC 3900 is a lenticular galaxy located in the Leo constellation. It was discovered by William Herschel in 1785. It is estimated to be about 95 to 100 million light-years away from Earth.

NGC3938.extract: NGC 3938 is an unbarred spiral galaxy in the Ursa Major constellation. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 6 February 1788.

NGC3941.extract: NGC 3941 is a barred lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of circa 40 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 3941 is about 40,000 light years across.

NGC3945.extract: NGC 3945 is a barred lenticular galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on March 19, 1790.

NGC3949.extract: NGC 3949 is an unbarred spiral galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major, approximately 50 million light-years away from the Earth. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 5 February 1788.

NGC3982.extract: NGC 3982 is an intermediate spiral galaxy approximately 68 million light-years away in the constellation Ursa Major. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on April 14, 1789, and misclassified as a planetary nebula. NGC 3982 is a part of the M109 Group.

NGC3992.extract: Messier 109 is a barred spiral galaxy in Ursa Major about 67.1 million light-years away, the most distant of the Messier galaxies assigned a specific identity. Its striking bar structure connects a bright nucleus to two main spiral arms, making it an excellent example of an SBb-type galaxy. Three dwarf satellite galaxies orbit it in a compact group.

NGC3998.extract: NGC 3998 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It was discovered on April 14, 1789, by German-British astronomer William Herschel. At a distance of 45 million light-years, it is located relatively nearby, making it a well-studied object.

NGC4026.extract: NGC 4026 is an edge-on lenticular galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of circa 50 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 4026 is about 80,000 light years across.

NGC4027.extract: NGC 4027 is a barred spiral galaxy approximately 83 million light-years away in the constellation Corvus. It is also a peculiar galaxy because one of its spiral arms goes out more than the other. This is probably due to a galactic collision in NGC 4027's past.

NGC4030.extract: NGC 4030 is a grand design spiral galaxy located about 64 million light years away in the constellation Virgo. It is a member of the NGC 4030 Group of galaxies, which is a member of the Virgo II Groups, a series of galaxies and galaxy clusters strung out from the southern edge of the Virgo Supercluster.

NGC4036.extract: NGC 4036 is the New General Catalogue identifier for a lenticular galaxy in the northern circumpolar constellation of Ursa Major. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 19 March 1790.

NGC4041.extract: NGC 4041 is the New General Catalogue identifier for a spiral galaxy in the northern circumpolar constellation of Ursa Major. It is located an estimated 70 million light years from the Sun. It was discovered by the German-British astronomer William Herschel on 19 March 1790.

NGC4051.extract: NGC 4051 is an intermediate spiral galaxy in the constellation of Ursa Major. It was discovered on 6 February 1788 by William Herschel.

NGC4088.extract: NGC 4088 is an intermediate spiral galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major. The galaxy forms a physical pair with NGC 4085, which is located 11′ away.

NGC4102.extract: NGC 4102 is an intermediate barred spiral galaxy located in the northern constellation of Ursa Major. The galaxy was discovered April 12, 1789 by William Herschel.

NGC4111.extract: NGC 4111 is a lenticular galaxy in the constellation Canes Venatici. It is located at a distance of circa 50 million light-years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 4111 is about 55,000 light-years across.

NGC4147.extract: NGC 4147 is the New General Catalogue identifier for a globular cluster of stars in the northern constellation of Coma Berenices. It was discovered by English astronomer William Herschel on March 14, 1784, who described it as "very bright, pretty large, gradually brighter in the middle".

NGC4150.extract: NGC 4150 is an elliptical galaxy located approximately 45 million light years away in the constellation Coma Berenices. It was discovered by William Herschel on March 13, 1785.

NGC4151.extract: The galaxy was first mentioned by William Herschel on March 17, 1787. It was one of the six galaxies described in Carl Seyfert's original paper which defined such systems.

NGC4179.extract: NGC 4179 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Virgo. It was discovered by William Herschel on January 14, 1784.

NGC4203.extract: NGC 4203 is the New General Catalogue identifier for a lenticular galaxy in the northern constellation of Coma Berenices. The morphological classification of NGC 4203 is SAB0−, indicating that it has a lenticular form with tightly wound spiral arms and a weak bar structure at the nucleus.

NGC4214.extract: NGC 4214 is a dwarf barred irregular galaxy located around 10 million light-years away in the constellation Canes Venatici. It was discovered on 28 April 1785 by German-British astronomer William Herschel. NGC 4214 is a member of the M94 Group.

NGC4245.extract: NGC 4245 is a barred lenticular galaxy with a ring located in the constellation Coma Berenices. It was discovered on March 13, 1785, by the astronomer William Herschel. It is a member of the Coma I Group.

NGC4258.extract: Messier 106 is a Seyfert 2 spiral galaxy in Canes Venatici about 23.7 million light-years away, famous for being one of a handful of galaxies with precisely measured distances using water maser emission from its active nucleus. The maser traces a nearly edge-on disk of gas orbiting a 36-million-solar-mass central black hole, allowing a geometric distance measurement independent of the cosmic distance ladder. Strikingly, it has two anomalous arms of hot, jet-driven gas spiraling outward from the nucleus visible in radio and X-ray images alongside two normal optical spiral arms.

NGC4261.extract: NGC 4261 is an elliptical galaxy located around 100 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on April 13, 1784.

NGC4273.extract: NGC 4273 is a barred spiral galaxy in the constellation of Virgo. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 17 April 1786.

NGC4274.extract: NGC 4274 is a barred spiral galaxy located in the constellation Coma Berenices. It is located at a distance of about 45 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 4274 is about 95,000 light years across.

NGC4278.extract: NGC 4278 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Coma Berenices. It is located at a distance of circa 55 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 4278 is about 65,000 light years across.

NGC4293.extract: NGC 4293 is a lenticular galaxy in the northern constellation of Coma Berenices. It was discovered by English astronomer William Herschel on March 14, 1784, who described it as "large, extended, resolvable, 6 or 7′ long".

NGC4303.extract: Messier 61 is a large face-on barred spiral in the southern Virgo Cluster, about 52.5 million light-years away. It has one of the highest supernova rates of any nearby galaxy, with six recorded supernovae since 1926. Its well-defined bar and multiple spiral arms make it one of the more photogenic Virgo Cluster spirals.

NGC4314.extract: NGC 4314 is a barred spiral galaxy approximately 53 million light-years away in the northern constellation of Coma Berenices. It is positioned around 3° to the north and slightly west of the star Gamma Comae Berenices and is visible in a small telescope.

NGC4346.extract: NGC 4346 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Canes Venatici about 50 million light-years away. It was discovered on April 1, 1788 by the astronomer William Herschel.

NGC4361.extract: NGC 4361 is a planetary nebula in the Corvus constellation. It is included in the Astronomical League's Herschel 400 Observing Program.

NGC4365.extract: NGC 4365 is a giant elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Virgo. It was discovered by German-English astronomer William Herschel on April 13, 1784. The galaxy is located at an estimated distance of 74.4 million light-years (22.80 Mpc) from the Milky Way galaxy.

NGC4394.extract: It was discovered on 14 March 1784 by the German–British astronomer William Herschel. It is a presumed companion to the lenticular galaxy M85 / NGC 4382, which lies 8 arc minutes away.

NGC4414.extract: NGC 4414, also known as the Dusty Spiral Galaxy, is an unbarred spiral galaxy about 62 million light-years away in the constellation Coma Berenices. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 13 March 1785.

NGC4419.extract: NGC 4419 is a barred spiral galaxy in the constellation of Coma Berenices. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 8 April 1784.

NGC4429.extract: NGC 4429 is a lenticular galaxy located about 55 million light-years away in the constellation of Virgo. NGC 4429 is tilted at an inclination of about 75° which means that the galaxy is tilted almost edge-on as seen from Earth.

NGC4435.extract: The Eyes Galaxies are a pair of galaxies about 52 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo. The pair are members of the string of galaxies known as Markarian's Chain.

NGC4438.extract: The Eyes Galaxies are a pair of galaxies about 52 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo. The pair are members of the string of galaxies known as Markarian's Chain.

NGC4442.extract: NGC 4442 also known as UGC 7583, is a barred lenticular galaxy, luminous infrared galaxy and active galaxy in the constellation of Virgo. The galaxy is located in the Virgo Cluster, along with other notable galaxies such as Messier 87 and the Eyes Galaxies.

NGC4448.extract: NGC 4448 is a barred spiral galaxy with a prominent inner ring structure in the constellation Coma Berenices.

NGC4450.extract: NGC 4450 is a spiral galaxy in the constellation Coma Berenices.

NGC4459.extract: NGC 4459 is a lenticular galaxy located about 50 million light-years away in the constellation of Coma Berenices. NGC 4459 is also classified as a LINER galaxy. NGC 4459 was discovered by astronomer William Herschel on January 14, 1787. NGC 4459 is a member of the Virgo Cluster.

NGC4473.extract: NGC 4473 is an elliptical galaxy located about 50 million light-years away in the constellation of Coma Berenices. It was discovered by astronomer William Herschel on April 8, 1784.

NGC4477.extract: NGC 4477 is a barred lenticular galaxy located about 55 million light-years away in the constellation of Coma Berenices. NGC 4477 is classified as a type 2 Seyfert galaxy.

NGC4478.extract: NGC 4478 is an elliptical galaxy located about 50 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo. NGC 4478 was discovered by astronomer William Herschel on April 12, 1784. NGC 4478 is a member of the Virgo Cluster.

NGC4494.extract: NGC 4494 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Coma Berenices. It is located at a distance of circa 45 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 4494 is about 60,000 light years across.

NGC4527.extract: NGC 4527 is a spiral galaxy in the constellation Virgo. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 23 February 1784.

NGC4535.extract: NGC 4535 is a barred spiral galaxy located some 54 million light years from Earth in the constellation Virgo. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 28 December 1785. Due to its low surface brightness, it has been called "The Lost Galaxy."

NGC4536.extract: NGC 4536 is an intermediate spiral galaxy in the constellation Virgo. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 24 January 1784.

NGC4546.extract: NGC 4546 is a lenticular field galaxy located in the direction of the constellation Virgo, with a total population of globular clusters estimated at 390. It is a member of the Virgo II Groups, a series of galaxies and galaxy clusters strung out from the southern edge of the Virgo Supercluster.

NGC4564.extract: NGC 4564 is an elliptical galaxy located about 57 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo. NGC 4564 was discovered by astronomer William Herschel on March 15, 1784. The galaxy is also a member of the Virgo Cluster.

NGC4570.extract: NGC 4570 is an edge-on lenticular galaxy located about 57 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo. NGC 4570 was discovered by astronomer William Herschel on April 13, 1784 and is a member of the Virgo Cluster.

NGC4596.extract: NGC 4596 is a barred lenticular galaxy located about 55 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo. NGC 4596 was discovered by astronomer William Herschel on March 15, 1784. NGC 4596 is a member of the Virgo Cluster and has an inclination of about 38°.

NGC4618.extract: NGC 4618 is a distorted barred dwarf galaxy in the constellation Canes Venatici. The galaxy is formally classified as a Sm galaxy, which means that its structure vaguely resembles the structure of spiral galaxies.

NGC4636.extract: NGC 4636 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Virgo. It is a member of the NGC 4753 Group of galaxies, which is a member of the Virgo II Groups, a series of galaxies and galaxy clusters strung out from the southern edge of the Virgo Supercluster.

NGC4643.extract: NGC 4643 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Virgo. It is a member of the NGC 4753 Group of galaxies, which is a member of the Virgo II Groups, a series of galaxies and galaxy clusters strung out from the southern edge of the Virgo Supercluster.

NGC4654.extract: NGC 4654 is an intermediate spiral galaxy in the constellation of Virgo at a distance of 55 million light years from the Milky Way that can be spotted with amateur telescopes.

NGC4660.extract: NGC 4660 is an elliptical galaxy located about 63 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo. The galaxy was discovered by astronomer William Herschel on March 15, 1784 and is a member of the Virgo Cluster.

NGC4666.extract: NGC 4666 is a spiral galaxy in the equatorial constellation of Virgo, located at a distance of approximately 55 megalight-years from the Milky Way. It was discovered by the German-born astronomer William Herschel on February 22, 1784.

NGC4689.extract: NGC 4689 is a spiral galaxy located about 54 million light-years away in the constellation of Coma Berenices. NGC 4689 is also classified as a LINER galaxy.

NGC4697.extract: NGC 4697 is a bright elliptical galaxy in Virgo about 54 million light-years away, notable for harboring an unusually large population of X-ray binary systems detected by the Chandra X-ray Observatory. Studies of its planetary nebulae population have provided one of the most precise kinematic measurements of a giant elliptical galaxy halo, showing evidence for a dark matter halo extending well beyond the optical body. It is one of the brighter ellipticals outside the Virgo Cluster proper.

NGC4699.extract: NGC 4699 is an intermediate spiral galaxy located in the constellation Virgo. It is located at a distance of about 65 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 4699 is about 85,000 light years across.

NGC4753.extract: NGC 4753 is a lenticular galaxy located about 60 million light-years away in the constellation of Virgo. NGC 4753 was discovered by astronomer William Herschel on February 22, 1784.

NGC4754.extract: NGC 4754 is a barred lenticular galaxy located about 53 million light-years away in the constellation of Virgo. NGC 4754 was discovered by astronomer William Herschel on March 15, 1784.

NGC4762.extract: NGC 4762 is an edge-on lenticular galaxy in the constellation Virgo. It is at a distance of 60 million light years and is a member of the Virgo Cluster.

NGC4772.extract: NGC 4772 is a spiral galaxy in the constellation Virgo. The galaxy lies about 90 million light years away from Earth based on redshift-independent methods, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 4772 is approximately 85,000 light years across.

NGC4781.extract: NGC 4781 is a barred spiral galaxy in the constellation Virgo. It was discovered by William Herschel on Mar 25, 1786.

NGC4845.extract: NGC 4845 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Virgo around 65 million light years away. The galaxy was originally discovered by William Herschel in 1786.

NGC4866.extract: NGC 4866 is an unbarred lenticular galaxy located roughly 100 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo. It was first observed by British astronomer Sir William Herschel on January 14, 1787. It is a member of the Virgo Cluster.

NGC4900.extract: NGC 4900 is a barred spiral galaxy in the constellation Virgo. It was discovered by William Herschel on April 30, 1786.

NGC4958.extract: NGC 4958 is a barred lenticular galaxy in the constellation Virgo. The galaxy lies about 40 million light years away from Earth based on refshift-independent methods, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 4958 is approximately 45,000 light years across.

NGC4995.extract: NGC 4995 is a "moderately bright and large galaxy" in the constellation Virgo. It is a member of the NGC 4995 Group of galaxies, which is a member of the Virgo II Groups, a series of galaxies and galaxy clusters strung out from the southern edge of the Virgo Supercluster.

NGC5054.extract: NGC 5054 is a spiral galaxy in the constellation Virgo. The galaxy lies about 55 million light years away from Earth based on redshift-independent methods, which means, given its apparent dimensions, that NGC 5054 is approximately 85,000 light years across.

NGC5084.extract: NGC 5084 is a Seyfert 2 lenticular galaxy in the constellation of Virgo. It is one of the largest and most massive galaxies in the Virgo Supercluster.

NGC5102.extract: NGC 5102, also known as Iota's Ghost since it appears near Iota Centauri in the sky, is a lenticular galaxy in the Centaurus A/M83 Group of galaxies. It was discovered by British astronomer John Herschel on 21 April 1835.

NGC5273.extract: NGC 5273 is a lenticular galaxy located 54 million light-years away in the northern constellation of Canes Venatici. This galaxy was discovered by William Herschel on May 1, 1785. It is positioned 1+1⁄4° to the southeast of the star 25 Canum Venaticorum.

NGC5322.extract: NGC 5322 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Ursa Major. It is located at a distance of circa 80 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 5322 is about 140,000 light years across.

NGC5363.extract: NGC 5363 is a lenticular galaxy located in the constellation Virgo. It is located at a distance of circa 65 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 5363 is about 100,000 light years across.

NGC5377.extract: NGC 5377 is an intermediate barred spiral galaxy located in the constellation Canes Venatici. NGC 5377 was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 12 May 1787.

NGC5473.extract: NGC 5473 is a lenticular galaxy in the constellation Ursa Major. It was discovered on April 14, 1789, by the astronomer William Herschel. Located roughly 85 million light-years away, it is part of a small galaxy group including NGC 5475 and NGC 5485.

NGC5557.extract: NGC 5557 is an elliptical galaxy in the constellation Boötes. It was discovered by William Herschel on May 1, 1785. The distance to NGC 5557 is not well known, but it is estimated to be about 127 million light-years away.

NGC5634.extract: NGC 5634 is a globular cluster in the constellation Virgo, located about 82,000 light years from Earth. NGC 5634 has an apparent magnitude of about 10 and a diameter of 4 or 5 arcminutes.

NGC5643.extract: NGC 5643 is an intermediate spiral galaxy in the constellation Lupus. Based on the tip of the red-giant branch distance indicator, it is located at a distance of about 40 million light-years. NGC 5643 has an active galactic nucleus and is a type II Seyfert galaxy.

NGC5676.extract: NGC 5676 is an unbarred spiral galaxy in the constellation Boötes.

NGC5694.extract: NGC 5694 is a compact, distant globular cluster in Hydra about 113,000 light-years from the Galactic Center, at the extreme outer fringe of the Milky Way halo. Studies of its radial velocity and orbit suggest it may be a captured interloper from an accreted dwarf galaxy, as its retrograde orbit is dynamically inconsistent with a native Milky Way globular. Its great distance makes it unresolvable in all but the largest amateur telescopes.

NGC5838.extract: NGC 5838 is a lenticular galaxy in the constellation Virgo, discovered by William Herschel in 1786. It is a member of the Virgo III Groups, a series of galaxies and galaxy clusters strung out to the east of the Virgo Supercluster of galaxies.

NGC5846.extract: NGC 5846 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Virgo. It is located at a distance of circa 90 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 5846 is about 110,000 light years across.

NGC5866.extract: The Spindle Galaxy is a lenticular galaxy in Draco about 44.9 million light-years away, seen nearly edge-on with a dark equatorial dust lane cutting through a prominent lens-shaped bulge. Its identity as Messier 102 was debated for nearly two centuries, with some arguing Messier intended to list NGC 5866 while others claimed it was a duplicate observation of M101. The dark lane and smooth stellar disk make it one of the more striking lenticular galaxies in moderate-aperture telescopes.

NGC5897.extract: NGC 5897 is a globular cluster in the constellation Libra. This satellite of the Milky Way, which is quite remote, has a diameter of over 170 light years. With its Shapley–Sawyer Concentration Class of XI, it has very low star density even in its center.

NGC5982.extract: NGC 5982 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Draco. It is located at a distance of about 130 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 5982 is about 100,000 light years across.

NGC6118.extract: NGC 6118 is a grand design spiral galaxy located 83 million light-years away in the constellation Serpens. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 14 April 1785.

NGC6144.extract: NGC 6144 is a globular cluster in the constellation Scorpius, located almost exactly 1° away from its brighter counterpart globular cluster Messier 4. It is relatively close to and is partially obscured by the Rho Ophiuchi cloud complex.

NGC6207.extract: NGC 6207 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Hercules. It is designated as SA(s)c in the galaxy morphological classification scheme and was discovered by William Herschel on 16 May 1787.

NGC6210.extract: It is a bright nebula. Due to its position, it has little extinction from intervening interstellar dust.

NGC6217.extract: NGC 6217 is a barred spiral galaxy located some 67 million light years away, in the constellation Ursa Minor. The galaxy is inclined by an angle of 33° to the line of sight along a position angle of 162°.

NGC6229.extract: NGC 6229 is a globular cluster located in the constellation Hercules. It is designated as GC(v)B in the galaxy morphological classification scheme and was discovered by the British astronomer William Herschel on 12 May 1787.

NGC6284.extract: NGC 6284 is a globular cluster located in the constellation Ophiuchus. It is designated as IX in the galaxy morphological classification scheme and was discovered by the German-born British astronomer William Herschel on 22 May 1784.

NGC6287.extract: NGC 6287 is a globular cluster located in the constellation Ophiuchus. It is designated as VII in the galaxy morphological classification scheme and was discovered by the German-born British astronomer William Herschel on 21 May 1784.

NGC6293.extract: NGC 6293 is a globular cluster located in the constellation Ophiuchus. Its Shapley–Sawyer Concentration Class is IV.

NGC6304.extract: NGC 6304 is a globular cluster in the constellation Ophiuchus. William Herschel discovered this star cluster using an 18.5-inch (47 cm) f/13 speculum reflector telescope in 1786. It is about 19,000 light-years away, near the Milky Way's central bulge.

NGC6316.extract: NGC 6316 is a globular cluster located in the constellation Ophiuchus. Its Shapley-Sawyer Concentration Class is III, meaning that it has a "strong inner core of stars" and was discovered by the German-born British astronomer William Herschel on 24 May 1784.

NGC6342.extract: NGC 6342 is a globular cluster located in the constellation Ophiuchus. Its Shapley–Sawyer Concentration Class is IV, and it was discovered by the German-born British astronomer William Herschel on 28 May 1786. It is at a distance of 28,000 light years away from Earth.

NGC6355.extract: NGC 6355 is a globular cluster located in the constellation Ophiuchus. It is at a distance of 28,000 light years away from Earth, and is currently part of the Galactic bulge.

NGC6356.extract: NGC 6356 is a globular cluster located in the constellation Ophiuchus. It is designated as a II in the Shapley–Sawyer Concentration Class and was discovered by the German-born British astronomer William Herschel on 18 June 1784.

NGC6369.extract: Little Ghost Nebula, also known as NGC 6369, is a planetary nebula in the constellation Ophiuchus. It was discovered by William Herschel in 1784.

NGC6401.extract: NGC 6401 is a globular cluster in the equatorial constellation of Ophiuchus. This star cluster was discovered by German-English astronomer William Herschel in 1784, but he mistakenly classified it as a bright nebula.

NGC6426.extract: NGC 6426 is a globular cluster of stars located in the equatorial constellation of Ophiuchus. It was discovered by the German-English astronomer William Herschel on 3 June 1786.

NGC6440.extract: NGC 6440 is a globular cluster of stars in the southern constellation of Sagittarius. It was discovered by German-English astronomer William Herschel on 28 May 1786.

NGC6520.extract: NGC 6520 is an open cluster of stars in the southern constellation of Sagittarius, about 4° to the east of the Galactic Center. Just to the west of this cluster is the dark nebula Barnard 86, dubbed the Ink Spot.

NGC6522.extract: NGC 6522 is a globular cluster of stars in the southern constellation of Sagittarius. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on June 24, 1784.

NGC6528.extract: NGC 6528 is a globular cluster in the constellation Sagittarius, and is listed in the New General Catalogue. It has an apparent magnitude of about 11 and a diameter of about 16 arcminutes, and its Shapley–Sawyer Concentration Class is V, containing stars of 16th magnitude and dimmer.

NGC6540.extract: NGC 6540 is a globular cluster of stars in the souther constellation Sagittarius, positioned about 4.66° away from the Galactic Center. It was discovered by German-British astronomer Wilhelm Herschel on May 24, 1784, with an 18.7-inch mirror telescope, who described the cluster a…

NGC6544.extract: NGC 6544 is a small globular cluster visible in the constellation Sagittarius. It is magnitude 7.5, diameter 1 arcminute. It is less than 1 degree southeast of Messier 8, the Lagoon Nebula.

NGC6553.extract: NGC 6553 is a globular cluster in the constellation Sagittarius. Its Shapley–Sawyer Concentration Class is XI, meaning the star concentration is very loose even at the center; it has stars of magnitude 20 and dimmer.

NGC6569.extract: NGC 6569 is a globular cluster in the constellation Sagittarius. It is about 2 degrees south east of Gamma2 Sagittarii.

NGC6572.extract: NGC 6572 is a planetary nebula in the constellation Ophiuchus. It was discovered in 1825 by the German astronomer Friedrich Georg Wilhelm von Struve.

NGC6584.extract: NGC 6584 is a globular cluster in the constellation Telescopium that lies near Theta Arae and is 45000 light-years distant.

NGC6624.extract: NGC 6624 is a globular cluster in the constellation Sagittarius. It was discovered on 24 June 1784 by the astronomer William Herschel.

NGC6629.extract: NGC 6629 is a planetary nebula in the constellation of Sagittarius, located above the "Teapot". The object formed when a star ejected its outer layers during the late stages of its evolution.

NGC6638.extract: NGC 6638 is a globular cluster in the constellation Sagittarius. It is magnitude 9.5 and diameter 2 arc minutes, class VI. It is a half degree east of Lambda Sagittarii. It is a member of the Milky Way.

NGC6642.extract: NGC 6642 is a globular cluster located 26,700 light-years from Earth, in the constellation Sagittarius. Many "blue stragglers" have been spotted in this globular, and it is known to be lacking in low-mass stars.

NGC6664.extract: NGC 6664 is an open cluster in Scutum. It is located near NGC 6649.

NGC6712.extract: NGC 6712 is a globular cluster that was probably discovered by Le Gentil on July 9, 1749 when investigating the Milky Way star cloud in Aquila. He described it as a "true nebula," in contrast to the open star cluster M11.

NGC6717.extract: NGC 6717 is a globular cluster in the constellation Sagittarius, and is a member of the Palomar Globular Clusters group. Palomar 9 was discovered by William Herschel on August 7, 1784. It is located about 7,300 parsecs away from Earth.

NGC6723.extract: NGC 6723, also known as the Chandelier Cluster, is a globular cluster in the constellation Sagittarius. It is a class VII cluster with stars of magnitude 14 and dimmer.

NGC6741.extract: NGC 6741, also known as the Phantom Streak Nebula, is located about 7000 light-years away in the constellation of Aquila.

NGC6745.extract: NGC 6745 is an irregular galaxy about 206 million light-years away in the constellation Lyra. It was discovered by French astronomer Édouard Stephan on 24 July 1879.

NGC6755.extract: NGC 6755 is an open cluster of stars in the equatorial constellation of Aquila, positioned about 3° to the east of the star Delta Aquilae. It was discovered by the Anglo-German astronomer William Herschel on July 30, 1785 and is located at a distance of 8,060 light years from the Sun.

NGC6756.extract: NGC 6756 is a small open star cluster in the constellation Aquila, close to NGC 6755.

NGC6760.extract: NGC 6760 is a globular cluster in the constellation Aquila. It may have contributed to the formation of the open cluster Ruprecht 127 during NGC 6760's passage through the galactic disk 71 million years ago.

NGC6790.extract: NGC 6790 is a young, compact planetary nebula with a high surface brightness located in the equatorial constellation of Aquila. Imaging by the Hubble Space Telescope shows elongated shells surrounding the central star.

NGC6823.extract: NGC 6820 is a small emission nebula near the open cluster NGC 6823 in Vulpecula. The reflection nebula and cluster are embedded in a large faint emission nebula called Sh 2-86. The whole area of nebulosity is often referred to as NGC 6820.

NGC6830.extract: NGC 6830, also known as the Poodle Cluster, is an open cluster in Vulpecula. It contains about 150 members. It was discovered by William Herschel in 1784.

NGC6834.extract: NGC 6834 is a young open cluster of stars located about 10,850 light years from the Sun in the constellation Cygnus. It was discovered on July 17, 1784, by Anglo-German astronomer William Herschel.

NGC6866.extract: NGC 6866 is a young open cluster of stars in the northern constellation Cygnus. It was discovered by German astronomer Caroline Herschel on 23 July 1783.

NGC6885.extract: NGC 6885 is a sparse open cluster in Vulpecula about 1,900 light-years away, centered near the yellow supergiant star 20 Vulpeculae. The cluster contains roughly 30 to 40 confirmed members spread across a loose, irregular pattern best appreciated at low magnification. Its estimated age of around 350 million years is consistent with the evolved, slightly cooler supergiants visible among its membership.

NGC6934.extract: NGC 6934 is a moderately bright globular cluster in Delphinus about 52,000 light-years from Earth. Its outer regions are well resolved into individual stars in moderate apertures, while the core remains concentrated and unresolved. With an estimated age of around 10 billion years, it is a member of the Milky Way's old halo population.

NGC6939.extract: NGC 6939 is an open cluster in the constellation Cepheus. It was discovered by William Herschel in 1798. The cluster lies 2/3° northwest from the spiral galaxy NGC 6946. The cluster lies approximately 4,000 light years away and it is over a billion years old.

NGC6940.extract: NGC 6940 is an open cluster in the constellation Vulpecula. It was discovered by William Herschel in 1784. The cluster is nearly a billion years old and it is located 2,500 light years away. It is considered the finest open cluster in the constellation.

NGC7008.extract: NGC 7008, also known as the Fetus Nebula, is a planetary nebula with a diameter of approximately 1 light-year located at a distance of 2800 light years in northern Cygnus. It was discovered by William Herschel in 1787, in Slough, England.

NGC7026.extract: NGC 7026, also known as the Cheeseburger Nebula, is a planetary nebula located 6000 light-years away, in the constellation of Cygnus. The central star of the planetary nebula has a spectral type of [WO3], indicating a spectrum similar to that of an oxygen-rich Wolf–Rayet star.

NGC7044.extract: NGC 7044 is an open cluster located in the Cygnus constellation. Containing approximately 742 stars, it was discovered by 18th century English astronomer William Herschel on 17 October 1786.

NGC7160.extract: NGC 7160 is an open cluster in the constellation Cepheus. It was discovered by William Herschel on November 9, 1789.

NGC7209.extract: NGC 7209 is an open cluster in the constellation Lacerta. It was discovered by William Herschel on 19 October 1787.

NGC7217.extract: NGC 7217 is an unbarred spiral galaxy in the constellation Pegasus.

NGC7243.extract: NGC 7243 is a sparse open cluster in Lacerta about 2,800 light-years away, containing roughly 40 brighter members spread across an area about 1 degree wide. Its stars are loosely arranged, making it a sweeping low-magnification binocular object. The cluster is estimated to be around 100 million years old, with several slightly evolved yellow and orange stars among its mostly blue-white membership.

NGC7448.extract: NGC 7448 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Pegasus. It is located at a distance of circa 80 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 7448 is about 60,000 light years across.

NGC7510.extract: NGC 7510 is an open cluster of stars located around 11,400 light years away in the constellation Cepheus, near the border with Cassiopeia. This cluster forms part of the Perseus Spiral Arm.

NGC7606.extract: NGC 7606 is a spiral galaxy located in the constellation Aquarius. It is located at a distance of about 100 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 7606 is about 200,000 light years across.

NGC7619.extract: NGC 7619 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Pegasus. NGC 7619 and NGC 7626 are the dominant and brightest members of the Pegasus galaxy cluster. Both of them were discovered by William Herschel on September 26, 1785.

NGC7626.extract: NGC 7626 is an elliptical galaxy located in the constellation Pegasus. It was discovered on September 26, 1785, by the German-British astronomer William Herschel.

NGC7686.extract: NGC 7686 is a moderately-sized open cluster in the constellation Andromeda, containing about 80 stars. At magnitude 5.6, it is an easy target for binoculars and small telescopes.

NGC7723.extract: NGC 7723 is a barred spiral galaxy located in the constellation Aquarius. It is located at a distance of about 90 million light years from Earth, which, given its apparent dimensions, means that NGC 7723 is about 95,000 light years across.

NGC7727.extract: NGC 7727 is a peculiar galaxy in the constellation Aquarius. It harbors two galactic nuclei, each containing a supermassive black hole, separated 1,600 light years apart. It was discovered by German-British astronomer William Herschel on 27 November 1785.

NGC7790.extract: NGC 7790 is a young open cluster of stars located some 10,800 light years away from Earth in the northern constellation of Cassiopeia. NGC 7790 has a Trumpler class rating of II2m and the estimated age is 60–80 million years.
